coredata.dc:title: Effect of forging and heat treatment on the microstructure of in situ TiC/Ti-1100 composites
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Fengcang
Lu, Weijie
Qin, Jining
Zhang, Di
Ji, Bo
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, 1vol.% TiC/Ti-1100 composite was fabricated using in situ technologies, and the β transus temperature of the composite was tested by metallographic techniques. The ingots after breakdown were forged in various temperature ranges, i.e. in the β phase field, in the (α+β) phase field, started in the β phase field and finished in the (α+β) phase field and in the upper α field. Microstructure of the composite after this four type forgings was studied by optical microscopy (OM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Silicide precipitated from the present composites was studied using TEM and energy diffraction spectrum (EDS). It was found that there is an increase about 100K in β transus temperature compared to monolithic Ti-1100 alloy. Different typical microstructures were obtained after forging in different temperature ranges. Long axis direction of shuttle-shaped silicide has geometric connection with the direction of laths of α phase.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Forging
Microstructure
In situ titanium matrix composites
Silicides

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The β transus temperature of TiC/Ti-1100composites
Microstructure of the composite after forging at various temperatures

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to acknowledge a financial support provided by A Foundation for the Author of National Excellent Doctoral Dissertation of PR China under Grant No: 200332, the Research Fund of Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality under Grant No: 04DZ14002, 0452 nm045, 03ZR14063, and the ItoYama Foundation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: OMs of the composite after the samples quenched from: (a) 1373K; (b) 1383K.
Vertical section of Ti-8Al-C equilibrium alloy phase diagram and various forging temperature ranges.
OM of the composite in cast.

OMs of the composite after various forgings and heat treatments (the direction marked on Figs is the metal flowing orientation during deformation): (a) A1 forging and cooling in water; (b) A2 forging and cooling in water; (c) A2 forging and cooling in air; (d) A3 forging and cooling in water; (e) A4 forging and cooling in air; (f) A4 forging plus recrystallized anneal at 900°C.
Bright field TEMs of α phase after A3 forging: (a) finer substructure near TiC particle; (b) coarser substructure.
TEMs of precipitated silicide: (a) bright field; (b) dark field.
Relationship between long axis direction of silicide and the direction of plates of α phase.
Schematic diagrams showing the relationship in crystal structure between silicide and α phase.
Contents of alloying elements in the composite (as-cast)
The quantitative analysis results of EDS

article.ce:title: Effect of forging and heat treatment on the microstructure of in situ TiC/Ti-1100 composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Metal Matrix Composites, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200030, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai No. 5 Steel Co. Ltd., Bao Steel Group, Shanghai 200940, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, 1vol.% TiC/Ti-1100 composite was fabricated using in situ technologies, and the β transus temperature of the composite was tested by metallographic techniques. The ingots after breakdown were forged in various temperature ranges, i.e. in the β phase field, in the (α+β) phase field, started in the β phase field and finished in the (α+β) phase field and in the upper α field. Microstructure of the composite after this four type forgings was studied by optical microscopy (OM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Silicide precipitated from the present composites was studied using TEM and energy diffraction spectrum (EDS). It was found that there is an increase about 100K in β transus temperature compared to monolithic Ti-1100 alloy. Different typical microstructures were obtained after forging in different temperature ranges. Long axis direction of shuttle-shaped silicide has geometric connection with the direction of laths of α phase.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium matrix composites (TMCs), reinforced with ceramic particles, have considerable potential for improving creep properties and service temperature and can be extensively applied in areas such as aerospace, advanced weapon systems and the automotive industry [1]. TMCs with better mechanical properties can be prepared by the in situ technique. In situ techniques overcome the shortcomings of traditional techniques, such as the problems of pollution of reinforcements and wettability between ceramic particles and matrix encountered in the casting technique. Therefore in situ synthesized TMCs have been widely studied [2–4]. However, because of the distribution of the reinforcement with high hardness in the soft matrix the machining property of these composites is inferior. It is difficult to machine a work-piece with complex configuration, and this defect restricts the application and development of the composites. In order to solve these problems, hot forging of materials can be used. Hot forging saves raw materials and reduces cost. On the other hand, Ti-1100 is a near α Ti alloy. Near α Ti alloys offer considerable scope for obtaining a variety of microstructures by thermomechanical processing (TMP) and heat treatment [5]. Alloy specific studies have demonstrated significant benefits of using thermomechanical processing to alter microstructure and mechanical properties of near α Ti alloys [5–10]. In bimodal microstructures containing equiaxed α grains and Widmanstatten (α+β) lath grains. Bania [6], Seagle [7] and Thiehsen [8] have reported that both primary and secondary creep rates increase with the amount of equiaxed α-phase in the microstructure. Widmanstatten microstructure, containing multiple colonies of α and β laths within a prior β grain, is more creep resistant than the bimodal microstructure [9]. In addition, for Ti-1100 alloy, silicides can easily precipitate in the β phase film between α platelets during aging or in the application process, and the precipitation of silicides between α platelets is thought to be helpful for the creep resistance of the alloy [10].
The aim of this research is to investigate the effect of forging and heat treatment on the microstructure of the in situ TiC/Ti-1100 composite.

The material used in this experiment was a Ti-1100 alloy reinforced with TiC (1vol.%) ceramic particle. The nominal alloy composition of Ti-1100 was Ti–6Al–2.75Sn–4Zr–0.4Mo–0.45Si, and the amount of carbon added into the present composite was 0.25wt.%. The composite was prepared in a consumable vacuum arc remelting furnace (VAR) utilized the reaction between Ti and C elements. After casting, contents of alloying element in the composite were measured by chemical method, and the results are presented in Table 1 . Metallographic techniques were used to test the β transus temperature of the composite. The samples for βt temperature tests were cubes with a gauge size of 10mm×10mm×10mm. The samples were heated in a furnace with a temperature accuracy ±2K and protected by N2 gas, after hold for 30min then quenched into water. Firstly, tests were conducted with a temperature interval of 30K, and the β transus temperature of the composite was determined in the temperature range 1373–1403K. Secondly, tests were conducted with a temperature interval of 10K in the temperature range 1373–1403K. The ingots homogenization was performed at 1423K for 1.5h and cooled in air. The forging of the composite was performed using a hydraulic pressure machine with the molds heated to 1073K, and the temperature of samples during forging was measured using infrared ray techniques. The sample for compression was a column with 120mm in diameter. Strain rate during compression was 0.1s−1. The amount of deformation was calculated as the total reduction in cross section A 0/A i, where A 0 is the cross sectional area before hot forging and A i is the cross sectional area after hot forging. The samples after forging were quenched into water. Samples for optical microscopy (OM) were cut from hot-forging rods. Then the samples were prepared using conventional techniques of grinding and mechanical polishing. The samples were etched in Kroll's reagent (composition: 1–3ml HF, 2–6ml HNO3, 100ml water). The microstructure of samples was characterized using a LECO2000 microscope. Thin foils for transmission electron microscopy were prepared by twin-jet electro-polishing technique using an electrolyte composed of 10 pct HclO4 and 90 pct CH3COOH in a temperature range 293–303K and a potential of 30–50V. The foils were examined using a JEM-2010 electron microscope operating at 200kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The β transus temperature of TiC/Ti-1100composites
ce:section.ce:para: The β transus temperature is a very important parameter for the forging of Ti alloys and its composites. C element is one of α phase stabilizers for Ti alloys, and C element addition will increase the β transus temperature of the matrix alloy. Metallographic techniques were used to test the β transus temperature of the composite, and optical micrographs of the composite after quenched are presented in Fig. 1 . The β transus temperature of the present composite was identified as 1378K. There is an increase about 100K in βt compared to Ti-1100 (βt temperature is about 1288K [10]).

To interpret the obtained results, it is helpful to examine the Ti–Al–C ternary phase diagram. The present matrix alloys contain α stabilizers Al, Sn, Zr, and O, the effect of which can be represented in terms of an Al equivalent content. According to Rosenberg [12], the equivalent of Al content can be expressed as [Al]eq =[Al]+[Sn]/3+[Zr]/6+10[O]. From Table 1, for the present matrix alloy [Al]eq is calculated to be 8.3. Thus we can refer to the Ti–8Al–C phase system to discuss the (α+β)/β transus temperature of the matrix alloy under study. A vertical section of the Ti–8Al–C phase diagram [11] is shown in Fig. 2 , and the present composition of the composite is marked with A in Fig. 2. According to this vertical section, the addition of carbon increases the (α+β)/β transus temperature rapidly when the C concentration in matrix alloy is below 0.28wt.%, and the β transus temperature of the composite identified in this experiment is lower slightly than that presented in the Ti–8Al–C ternary phase diagram, which may result from Mo and Si in matrix which are β stabilizers. According to the results of β transus temperature identified in this experiment and Fig. 2, four type forgings were conducted to the composite, and the parameters used in this four type forgings are listed in Table 2 . The four type forging temperature ranges used in this experiment are also presented in Fig. 2, and A 1, A 2, A 3 and A 4 are usually named forging in the β phase field, forging in the (α+β) phase field, conventional forging and forging in the upper α phase field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure of the composite after forging at various temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: OM of the composite in cast is presented in Fig. 3 . From Fig. 3, it can be seen that the microstructure of the composite in cast consists of coarser dendritic grains. OMs of the composites after forging at various temperatures are presented in Fig. 4 . From Fig. 4(a), it can be seen that the Widmanstatten microstructure was obtained after A 1 forging. From Fig. 4(a), it can be know that the composite is in the β phase field during A 1 forging, and it is well known that the microstructure of near α Ti alloys is the Widmanstatten microstructure after forging in the β phase field. As Fig. 4(b) presented, the bimodal microstructure is obtained after A 2 forging, and the bimodal microstructure is usually obtained after forging in the (α+β) phase field. The microstructure presented in Fig. 4(c) is different from common bimodal microstructure (Fig. 4(b)), which is resulted from the different α phase precipitation mechanism during cooling process after forging. In the case of the microstructure presented in Fig. 4(b), due to the higher cooling rate, α phase nucleate in the β phase and grow rapidly, and the laths of precipitated α phase are finer; but in the case of Fig. 4(c), because of the lower cooling rate, α phase would precipitate with the primary α phase as nucleation, and the laths of precipitated α phase are coarser. The microstructure presented in Fig. 4(d) is different from either the Widmanstatten microstructure or the bimodal microstructure. The character of this microstructure is very inhomogeneous, which is resulted from the precipitation of α phase during forging process. When the temperature is below β transus temperature during A 3 forging, α phase will precipitate and take part in the deformation. The α phase that precipitated at lower temperature will not take part in the deformation until the α phase that precipitated at higher temperature have a large amount of deformation. So amount of deformation in adjacent α phase may be different, and the composite microstructure is inhomogeneous. As Fig. 4(e) presented, the microstructure after A 4 forging consists of aligned α plates that have been narrowed and aligned along the metal flowing orientation during forging. As Fig. 4(f) presented, the microstructure after A 4 forging plus recrystallized anneal treatment consists of near equiaxed α grains, which confirms that recrystallization have taken place in the α phase. Because the interface between adjacent α laths in a colony is a small-angled grain boundary, the interface between α laths could not impede the growth of new recrystallized α grains, and the recrystallized α grains can grow lager than the width of α laths easily. α Titanium deforms by slip on basal and prismatic planes during forging, which gives rise to substructures in the α phase [13]. On the other hand, TiC particles may impede the movement of dislocations in the α phase, which is helpful for the formation of the substructures in the α phase. The TEM of the substructures in α phase are presented in Fig. 5 . From Fig. 5, it can be seen that the substructures near TiC particles (Fig. 5(a)) are finer than that in other places (Fig. 5(b)).

TEMs of the silicide precipitated from the composite after aging at 923K are presented in Fig. 6 . It was reported that different types of sillicides precipitated during aging of the solution treated alloys depending upon composition of the alloys and heat treatment parameters [10]. The precipitation of two types silicides designated as (TiZr)5Si3 (S1, hexagonal crystal structure, a =0.78nm and c =0.54nm) and (TiZr)6Si3 (S2, hexagonal crystal structure, a =0.701nm and c =0.369nm) in Ti alloys containing Zr [10]. EDS of the silicide precipitated from the present composite are presented in Fig. 7 . The quantitative analysis result of EDS is presented in Table 3 , which indicates that the silicide precipitated in these experiments is consent to the S2 type. It is often found that the long axis direction of shuttle-shaped precipitated silicide intersects the stretching direction of α the platelets by about a 60° angle, as seen in Fig. 8 . This geometric connection is thought to mainly result from the specific crystal structure relationship that exists between S2 and α matrix. The orientation relationship that exists between S2 and α matrix is that
( 2
1 ¯
1 ¯
0 )
α
/ /
( 0
1
1 ¯
0 )
S 2
[12] (the microstructure of the samples after quenched was α′ phase. Because α′ phase and α phase have the same crystal structure and their lattice parameters constants are very similar, α is used to substitute α′ in expression in this paper). The precipitation of silicide is a process that consists of nucleation and growth procedures. Silicide can easily precipitate on the β phase film between α platelets and in α platelets where there are structure defects. The reason may be that such places are helpful to nucleate in term of energy. The following schematic diagram (Fig. 9 ) can be used to explain the observed phenomena. Both α and S2 phase have hexagonal crystal structure, but the lattice parameter of the a axis of S2 phase is approximately twice of that of α matrix (α phase: a =0.295nm, c =0.473nm). If the crystal lattice of S2 phase revolves clockwise by a 90° angle relative to the α crystal lattice, then the two phases satisfy the above orientation relationship. In the directions of
〈 1
1
2 ¯
0 〉
, there are the shortest lattice distances between the two phases. The lattice distances of S2 (d =1.214nm) is about four times of that of α (d =0.295nm) along this orientation, and the relative difference between them is about 11.57%. Therefore if α platelets grow along one of the
〈 1
1
2 ¯
0 〉
directions (a1 in Fig. 9), S2 phase will nucleate as Fig. 9 and grow preferentially along the other
〈 1
1
2 ¯
0 〉
directions (a2 or a3 in Fig. 9) so as to reduce the strain energy caused by the large difference between the a axis lattice parameters of the two phases. So the growing direction of α platelets just makes a 60° angle with the growth direction of the S2 phase.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Solid solution C element in matrix increases β transus temperature of in situ TiC/Ti-1100 composites rapidly. For in situ 1vol.% TiC/Ti-1100 composite, there is an increase about 100K in the β transus temperature compared to monolithic Ti-1100 alloy.

Different microstructures are obtained after forging in different phase fields. Widmanstatten microstructure is obtained after forging in the β phase field; bimodal microstructure is obtained after forging in the (α+β) phase field; due to the precipitation of α phase during forging, inhomogeneous bimodal microstructure is obtained after forging started in the β phase field and finished in the (α+β) phase field; the microstructure which consists of curved plates of α phase is obtained after forging in the upper α phase field; the microstructure which consists of equiaxed α grains is obtained after forging in the upper α phase field plus recrystallized anneal treatment.
TiC particles impede the movement of dislocations in the α phase, which is helpful for the formation of the substructures in the deformed α phase.

Silicide precipitated from the present composite after aging at 923K is according to S2 type. Long axis direction of shuttle-shaped silicide often has geometric connection with the direction of plates of α phase. The phenomenon was explained by the crystal structure relationship between them.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to acknowledge a financial support provided by A Foundation for the Author of National Excellent Doctoral Dissertation of PR China under Grant No: 200332, the Research Fund of Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality under Grant No: 04DZ14002, 0452nm045, 03ZR14063, and the ItoYama Foundation.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Handbook of Ternary Alloy Phase Diagrams
The Science, Technology and Application of Titanium
